hook: 215 steps tiled in ceramics from 60-odd countries by one obsessive Chilean
facts: Begun 1990 by Chilean painter Jorge Selarón as a tribute to the Brazilian people
215 steps clad in over 2,000 tiles collected and sent from more than 60 countries
Selarón found dead on the steps on 10 January 2013; ruled a suicide after early suspicion
Straddles the Lapa and Santa Teresa neighbourhoods
legend: Selarón claimed a recurring pregnant African woman painted on the red tiles held a private meaning he never explained to anyone
w: A broke Chilean painter started tiling the crumbling steps outside his door in 1990 and never stopped, begging and buying ceramics from over 60 countries, 2,000-plus of them, calling it his gift to Brazil. He was found dead here in 2013, ruled suicide. A staircase that ate a man's whole life.

hook: Granite slope where samba was born, in the heart of Little Africa
facts: Stone slope at the foot of Morro da Conceição, named for salt once unloaded and hauled here
Heart of the 'Little Africa' quarter settled by formerly enslaved and Bahian migrants
Certified a quilombo in 2005, with descendants' association still resident
Donga, Pixinguinha, Joao da Baiana and other founders played in its yards
legend: They say the granite was carved smooth by the salt-sacks dragged up it, and that the first true sambas were improvised on these steps before the word existed
w: A bare granite slope where dockworkers once hauled salt and where, in the tenements of Little Africa, the men who invented samba gathered to play. Certified a quilombo in 2005. Every Monday night the roda still crowds the stones. The street where a whole music started, and stayed.

hook: Excavated stone wharf where some 900,000 enslaved Africans first stepped ashore
facts: Built 1811 as the landing point for enslaved Africans arriving in South America
Paved over and lost, then rediscovered in 2011 during port redevelopment digs
Inscribed a UNESCO World Heritage site in 2017 as a memory of the slave trade
Excavation turned up amulets and worship objects from Congo, Angola and Mozambique
w: An estimated 900,000 enslaved Africans first touched this continent on these very stones from 1811. The wharf was buried, forgotten, then dug back up in 2011, amulets and offerings still in the mud. Now UNESCO-listed ground people leave flowers on. The most haunted pavement in Brazil.

hook: Manueline reading hall walled floor-to-vault in carved Portuguese books
facts: Founded 1837 by Portuguese immigrants; the hall inaugurated 1887 by Princess Isabel
Neo-Manueline facade carved in Lisbon stone and shipped across the Atlantic
Holds the largest collection of Portuguese works outside Portugal, some 350,000 volumes
Among its rarities a 1572 first edition of Camoes' Os Lusiadas
w: Homesick Portuguese traders built themselves a Lisbon in miniature in 1887, the facade carved in Portugal and shipped over, the reading hall rising three galleries of dark wood crammed with 350,000 books under a stained skylight. A copy of Camoes from 1572 sits inside. Vertigo, in a library.

hook: Belle-epoque tearoom of Antwerp mirrors where Rio's republic plotted over cake
facts: Opened 1894 by Portuguese immigrants on Rua Goncalves Dias
Halls hung with vast crystal mirrors brought from Antwerp, framed in jacaranda
Haunt of writers and composers including Olavo Bilac, Villa-Lobos and Chiquinha Gonzaga
Upper tearoom added 1922 under a stained-glass ceiling
w: Since 1894 the mirrors here have thrown light back and forth across a room where Villa-Lobos and the poets of the old republic took their afternoon tea. Belgian crystal, jacaranda frames, a stained-glass ceiling upstairs. Order the cake and sit where the belle epoque refuses to end.

hook: 42 white colonial arches that carried water, now carry a tram
facts: Carioca Aqueduct built c.1723-1744 to bring water from the Santa Teresa hills
Two tiers of 42 stone arches reaching 17.6 metres, spanning some 270 metres
Decommissioned as an aqueduct in the late 19th century
Since then it has carried the Santa Teresa tram across to the hill
w: Forty-two whitewashed arches striding two storeys across Lapa, built by the 1740s to drag fresh water down from Santa Teresa's springs. The water stopped flowing over a century ago; now the little yellow tram rattles across the top instead. Colonial plumbing turned into a viaduct turned into theatre.

hook: Roofless shell of a salon hostess's mansion, now glass-and-steel and open sky
facts: Former home of Belle Epoque art patron Laurinda Santos Lobo (1878-1946)
Hosted artists and intellectuals at lavish gatherings in the early 20th century
Left to ruin for some 40 years after her death
Reopened 1997 as a cultural centre, modern walkways threaded through the bare brick
w: Laurinda Santos Lobo threw the parties that mattered here, hosting the artists of Belle Epoque Rio until she died in 1946 and the house slid into 40 years of ruin. Architects later laced steel walkways through the roofless brick rather than rebuild it. You climb a dead salon for the best view in Santa Teresa.

hook: Plain hilltop facade hiding a cave of 18-karat gilded baroque
facts: Benedictine abbey founded 1590; church built between 1617 and 1641 on Morro de Sao Bento
Interior carved in rosewood and lavishly gilded, much in 18-karat gold
Carving begun by monk Domingos da Conceicao, later reworked by Inacio Ferreira Pinto
Sunday mass still sung in Gregorian chant with organ
w: The street facade gives nothing away. Step inside and the whole interior detonates in gold, walls of rosewood drowned in 18-karat gilding by monk-carvers across two centuries. Benedictines have held the hill since 1590 and still sing Gregorian chant on Sunday mornings. Austerity outside, delirium within.

hook: Lime-green neo-Gothic customs castle that hosted the empire's last ball
facts: Neo-Gothic palace on an island in Guanabara Bay, inaugurated 1889 under Pedro II
Built as a customs house, inspired by the work of Viollet-le-Duc
Hosted the Baile da Ilha Fiscal on 9 November 1889, the last ball of the Empire
Six days later the monarchy fell; now run by the Brazilian Navy
w: A fairy-tale castle in lime green, built as a customs post and opened in 1889, that threw the most famous party in Brazilian history that November, the Empire's last ball. Six days later a coup ended the monarchy. The dancers had no idea. A green folly marooned at the exact end of an era.

hook: Brazil's oldest working cemetery, dug for Protestants barred from holy ground
facts: Founded 1811 for British Protestants who could not be buried in Catholic churches
Granted under the 1810 Anglo-Portuguese treaty after the ports opened
First burial recorded 15 January 1811; oldest open cemetery still operating in Brazil
Tucked behind Central station, reached through the Gamboa tunnel
w: When the ports opened, British merchants flooded in, and being Protestant could not lie in Catholic ground, so in 1811 they were granted this terraced hillside behind the station. The oldest cemetery still working in Brazil, all weathered English names and sea-captains. You reach it through a tunnel, fittingly.

hook: Sculpture forest grown over a cleared favela above the lagoon
facts: Hillside above Lagoa Rodrigo de Freitas, once the Catacumba favela
Community removed and the slope reforested from 1970
Opened 1979 as Brazil's first permanent open-air sculpture park
Trails climb to the Sacopa lookout over the lagoon and Pedra da Gavea
legend: The name 'catacumba', catacomb, supposedly recalls an old indigenous burial ground once on the slope
w: A favela of thousands clung to this slope above the lagoon until it was cleared from 1970 and planted over. In 1979 it reopened seeded with monumental sculpture, Brazil's first open-air park of the kind. Legend ties the name, catacomb, to an old burial ground. Art grown over an erased neighbourhood.

hook: Pastel neo-colonial square fed by a river, salvaged from demolished old Rio
facts: Small square enclosed by neocolonial houses in the wooded Cosme Velho valley
Several houses rebuilt with colonial materials salvaged from demolitions elsewhere
Reached by the Beco do Boticario alley beside the Carioca river
First house begun 1831 by the godfather of writer Machado de Assis
legend: They say the square is named for a court apothecary who once lived here, and locals tell of it being haunted by figures from old Rio
w: A pocket of pastel colonial houses around a tiny square deep in a forested valley, the Carioca river running past, reached down an alley. Much of it was rebuilt in the 1920s from doors and stone salvaged off buildings being demolished across the city. Named, they say, for an old court apothecary.

hook: Latin America's oldest electric tram, climbing an aqueduct to the hill
facts: Running since 1877, electrified 1896, the oldest electric railway in Latin America
Crosses to Santa Teresa over the top of the 18th-century Arcos da Lapa
Suspended 2011 after a fatal derailment killed six
Returned to service from 2015, fully restored along the route by 2019
w: The little yellow bonde has clattered up to Santa Teresa since 1877, electrified by 1896, the oldest electric tram on the continent, crossing into the hill over the top of a colonial aqueduct. A 2011 derailment killed six and shut it for years. It runs again now, slower, wiser. Rio's stubborn survivor.

hook: Pink forest chapel that once held Portinari's panels deep in Tijuca
facts: Built 1855 as a private chapel on a coffee estate inside what is now Tijuca forest
Castro Maya commissioned four panels from Candido Portinari for it in 1943
Portinari's originals now held by the National Museum of Fine Arts; reproductions on site
Stands at about 460 metres near the Alto da Boa Vista park entrance
w: A small pink chapel from 1855, left over from a coffee estate, now sitting alone in the rainforest of Tijuca. In 1943 Portinari painted four panels for it; the originals went to a museum and copies hang in their place. You climb through replanted jungle to find a chapel where a master once worked. Improbable, and quiet.

hook: 700-metre corridor of imperial palms in a garden Dom João planted in 1808
facts: Botanical garden created by royal decree in 1808 to acclimatise spice plants
Avenue of 134 imperial palms running some 700 metres, planted in the late 19th century
Named for naturalist Joao Barbosa Rodrigues, director 1890-1909
The first palm, planted by Dom Joao in 1809, was felled by lightning in 1972
legend: Story has it the garden's keepers long guarded the imperial palm seeds jealously to stop rivals breaking the royal monopoly on the prized tree
w: Dom Joao seized an old sugar estate in 1808 to grow nutmeg and pepper, and planted a royal palm whose seedlings became this corridor, 134 of them marching 700 metres skyward. Lightning killed the original tree in 1972; its trunk still stands. They say the seeds were once guarded like crown jewels.

hook: Babylonian basement hall of glazed tiles under an opera house
facts: Opera house inaugurated 1909 facing Cinelandia square
Foyer dome and curtain painted by Eliseu Visconti; stained glass made in Stuttgart
Basement Salao Assirio clad in glazed pottery, columns capped with Persian bull-heads
Decor inspired by ancient Babylon, said to be unique in South America
w: Above ground, a Paris-style opera house from 1909 with a Visconti dome. Below it, down the stairs, a wholly different fever dream: a hall sheathed in glazed tile and crowned with Persian bull-head columns, ancient Babylon transplanted to a Rio basement. Cariocas dined under the winged bulls. Opera upstairs, Mesopotamia down.

hook: Sea-level path under Sugarloaf where wild monkeys ignore the climbers
facts: Paved 1.25 km trail hugging the rock at the foot of Sugarloaf, beside Praia Vermelha
Named for the 1978 World Cup coach who promoted fitness running in Brazil
A side path branches up to the first peak, Morro da Urca, on foot
Threads recovering Atlantic forest full of marmosets and seabirds
w: Skip the cable car queue and walk instead: a flat path squeezed between the granite of Sugarloaf and the open Atlantic, named for a football coach who taught Brazil to jog. Marmosets pick over the rocks, indifferent. A side trail scrambles up to the first peak for free. The locals' way up the famous mountain.

hook: Presidential palace preserving the bedroom where Vargas shot himself
facts: Mansion built 1858-1867, Brazil's presidential palace 1897-1960
President Getulio Vargas shot himself here on 24 August 1954
Now the Museu da Republica; the third-floor bedroom kept as it was
His bullet-holed pyjama top displayed under glass
w: For six decades Brazil's presidents lived in this palace, until the morning of 24 August 1954, when Getulio Vargas climbed to his third-floor bedroom and shot himself in the chest. The room is kept as it was; his pyjama top, bullet hole and all, lies under glass. A presidential house and a crime scene at once.

hook: Cobbled Little Africa square watched over by samba's foremothers in mural
facts: Square of eclectic 1902 townhouses at the foot of Morro da Conceicao
Part of the historic Little Africa port quarter, steps from Pedra do Sal
Murals here honour figures including samba matriarch Tia Ciata
On the Circuit of Celebration of African Heritage marked through the port zone
w: A cobbled square of 1902 townhouses at the foot of Morro da Conceicao, in the old port quarter they called Little Africa. Murals here keep watch over the place, among them Tia Ciata, whose lost house nearby cradled the first sambas. Bars spill out on weekend nights. Memory, painted on the walls.

hook: Rio's oldest church, where single women still beg the matchmaker saint
facts: Franciscans took the hill in 1608; first mass said 1615, the city's oldest church
Celebrated as the oldest church in Rio, atop Morro de Santo Antonio
Sacristy of around 1714 reckoned the most beautiful in the city
Santo Antonio venerated across Brazil as the saint of marriage and lost things
legend: Brazilian women still pray to Santo Antonio for a husband, some turning his statue upside down or dunking it in water until he delivers one
w: Franciscans have held this hilltop since 1608, making it the oldest church in Rio, its 1714 sacristy reckoned the loveliest in the city. Santo Antonio doubles as Brazil's matchmaker saint: the lovelorn still pray for a husband here, and legend says some turn his statue upside down until he delivers. Faith, and a little blackmail.
